raw_case:
Case Report: A Chinese child with Barth syndrome caused by a novel TAFAZZIN mutation
Barth syndrome (BTHS) is a rare X-linked recessive genetic disorder characterized by a broad spectrum of clinical features including cardiomyopathy, skeletal myopathy, neutropenia, growth delay, and 3-methylglutaconic aciduria. This disease is caused by loss-of-function mutations in the TAFAZZIN gene located on chromosome Xq28, resulting in cardiolipin deficiency. Most patients are diagnosed in childhood, and the mortality rate is highest in the early years. We report a case of acute, life-threatening metabolic decompensation occurring one day after birth. A novel TAFAZZIN splice site mutation was identified in the patient, marking the first reported case of such a mutation in BTHS identified in China. The report aims to expand our understanding of the spectrum of TAFAZZIN mutations in BTHS.1

Introduction

BTHS (MIM 302060) was initially identified as a rare X-linked recessive genetic disorder primarily affecting the mitochondrial function in neutrophils, as well as cardiac and skeletal muscles. The syndrome is characterized by cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, developmental delays, skeletal muscle dysfunction, and 3-methylglutaconic aciduria (1). The causative factor of BTHS has been traced to loss-of-function mutations in the TAFAZZIN gene located on the Xq28 region of the X chromosome (2). This study reports a Chinese pediatric case of BTHS, which is the first reported case of this specific novel splice site mutation. This discovery enhances our understanding of the molecular mechanisms underlying the disease.2

Case presentation

The male neonate was delivered via cesarean section at 36 weeks of gestation due to fetal cardiac anomalies and umbilical cord entanglement. He weighed 2,780 g at birth, with Apgar scores of 9 at 1, 5, and 10 min. His condition deteriorated rapidly after delivery, with severe cyanosis, frothing, and reduced vitality. Initial vital signs demonstrated no fever, low blood pressure (65/29 mmHg), tachycardia (heart rate 128–152 beats/min), or rapid breathing (respiratory rate 52–62 breaths/min), with oxygen saturation at 91%–95%. Chest radiography revealed cardiomegaly (Figure 1A). Concurrent echocardiography showed left atrial and ventricular enlargement with thickening of the ventricular wall and septum, with a left ventricular internal diameter of 1.9 cm and a wall thickness of 0.5 cm. The left ventricular ejection fraction was 36%, indicating decreased contractility, but there was no noncompaction of the LV myocardium (Figure 1B). Blood tests revealed metabolic acidosis (pH 7.21, PCO2 59 mmHg, HCO3–0.60 mmol/L), coagulation abnormalities (activated partial thromboplastin time 3.12), creatine kinase levels at 438 U/L, and brain natriuretic peptide levels at 15,638 pg/ml, with normal troponin levels. The neutrophil count was 1.36*109/L. Based on these clinical manifestations, BTHS was suspected. Treatment included continuous low-flow nasal cannula oxygen and anti-infective therapy with piperacillin sodium, tazobactam sodium, sodium creatine phosphate, and oral digoxin to enhance myocardial function. The patient's cardiac function improved significantly, and he was eventually discharged. Subsequent whole-exome sequencing was performed to identify mutations in the couple and their children, revealing a novel DNA splicing site mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.778 -30_778del) (Figure 1C). The mutation was inherited from a mother with no cardiac disease history. According to the American College of Medical Genetics and Genomics criteria (Pathogenic very strong 1 and Moderate 2), this novel variant was likely pathogenic.3

Discussion

BTHS is an X-linked recessive genetic disorder first described by Barth et al. (3) characterized by cardiomyopathy, skeletal myopathy, neutropenia, growth delay, and increased urinary excretion of 3-methylglutaconic acid (3). Additional features include dilation, hypertrophy, and incomplete compaction of the left ventricle, leading to congestive heart failure. Early studies indicated high mortality rates in infants and children (4). Molecular genetic testing for mutations in TAFAZZIN has been used to diagnose BTHS, with over 120 pathogenic mutations identified across all 11 exons and introns of the gene (5). Nicola et al. described younger cousins with dilated cardiomyopathy including myocardial thickening, excessive trabeculation, mild-to-moderate mitral valve insufficiency, and impaired left ventricular contractile function with an ejection fraction of 38%. Older cousins exhibited mild clinical features. A hemizygous DNA splice site mutation (c.777+1G>A) was identified in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (6) among these cousins. TAE et al. also reported a 13-month-old boy with refractory heart failure attributed to dilated cardiomyopathy due to hemi-frame shift deletion of nine amino acids within exon 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.725_751del, p.Pro242_Glu250del) (7). Similar to the results of our study, these reports indicate that TAFAZZIN mutations may affect cardiac metabolism and dysfunction. Laure et al. reported the first confirmed case of BTHS in a female patient who experienced severe heart failure at one month old diagnosed with dilated, hypo contractile, and hypertrophic cardiomyopathy, with incomplete contraction of the left ventricle (8). Then, a large deletion encompassing the first five exons of TAFAZZIN was confirmed through genetic analysis. BTHS is caused by mutations in TAFAZZIN located on chromosome Xq28, which encodes tafazzin, a protein involved in the remodeling of cardiolipin, a phospholipid vital for mitochondrial membrane integrity. Tafazzin, a phospholipid transacylase, is involved in the remodeling of phosphatidylglycerol and cardiolipin within the inner mitochondrial membrane. Defects in tafazzin enzyme activity lead to cardiolipin loss, disrupting the stability of respiratory chain supercomplexes, and impairing electron transport chain function (9–11). Recent studies have demonstrated that the genetic background of a mouse model of BTHS influenced the phenotypic expression of tafazzin, potentially altering mitochondrial quality control (12). A new mutation identified in China, a DNA splicing site mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.778-30_778del), aids in establishing the genotype-phenotype relationship of tafazzin induced BTHS and improves our understanding of its function.4

Conclusions

This report shows a splicing site mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.778-30_778del), which is the first genetically confirmed case of BTHS in a Chinese child.

generate_case.case_summary:
- **Patient Information:** Male neonate, Chinese ethnicity.
- **Chief Complaint:** Severe cyanosis, frothing, and reduced vitality within one day after birth.
- **History of Present Illness:** Rapid condition deterioration after birth with cardiac anomalies identified prenatally.
- **Past Medical History:** None.
- **Personal History:** Delivered via cesarean section at 36 weeks gestation due to fetal cardiac anomalies and umbilical cord entanglement.
- **Family History:** Mother carries the TAFAZZIN mutation but has no cardiac disease history.
- **Physical Examination:**
  - Severe cyanosis, frothing, reduced vitality.
  - Low blood pressure (65/29 mmHg), tachycardia (128-152 beats/min), rapid breathing (52-62 breaths/min), oxygen saturation at 91%–95%.
- **Ancillary Tests:** 
  - Chest radiography: cardiomegaly.
  - Echocardiography: Left atrial/ventricular enlargement, thickened ventricular wall and septum, reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (36%), no noncompaction.
  - Blood tests: Metabolic acidosis (pH 7.21), abnormal coagulation, elevated creatine kinase (438 U/L), high brain natriuretic peptide (15,638 pg/ml), low neutrophil count (1.36*10^9/L), normal troponin levels.
  - Genetic testing: Novel DNA splicing site mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.778-30_778del).
generate_case.differential_diagnosis:
- **Initial considerations included:** Neonatal cardiomyopathies, genetic mitochondrial disorders, infections, and metabolic diseases.
- **Reasons for considering Barth syndrome (BTHS):**
  - Clinical triad suggestive of BTHS (cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, and 3-methylglutaconic aciduria, though the latter was not explicitly tested here).
  - Echocardiographic and laboratory findings (dilated cardiomyopathy, low ejection fraction, metabolic acidosis, neutropenia).
- **Exclusion of other diagnoses:**
  - Infectious causes: No clinical or laboratory signs of active infection.
  - Non-BTHS cardiomyopathies lacked evidence of alternative specific genetic or acquired causes (e.g., myocarditis, idiopathic cardiomyopathy).
  - Rarity and specific mutation findings reinforced BTHS's likelihood.
- **Diagnostic confirmation:** Whole-exome sequencing revealed a novel pathogenic splicing mutation (c.778-30_778del) in the TAFAZZIN gene, inherited in an X-linked pattern.
generate_case.final_diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with Barth Syndrome (BTHS) caused by a novel TAFAZZIN splicing site mutation (c.778-30_778del), marking the first genetically confirmed case in a Chinese child. Clinical and genetic findings supported this conclusion.
generate_case.diagnosis_results: Barth Syndrome (BTHS) caused by a novel TAFAZZIN (c.778-30_778del) mutation.

results.thinking_process:
Okay, let me try to work through this case. The patient is a male neonate with severe cyanosis, frothing, and reduced vitality right after birth. He was delivered via C-section at 36 weeks because of prenatal findings of cardiac anomalies and umbilical cord entanglement. The family history is notable for the mother carrying a TAFAZZIN mutation, but she doesn't have cardiac disease herself. 

First, the main issue here is the severe cyanosis. In a neonate, cyanosis could be due to cardiac or respiratory causes. The frothing might suggest pulmonary issues, maybe something like surfactant deficiency, but given the prenatal cardiac anomalies, a congenital heart defect is high on the list. However, the echocardiography didn't mention structural defects like transposition or tetralogy of Fallot, which are common cyanotic heart diseases. Instead, the echo shows left atrial and ventricular enlargement, thickened walls, and reduced ejection fraction. That makes me think more about cardiomyopathy rather than a structural heart defect.

The TAFAZZIN mutation is a big clue here. I remember that mutations in the TAFAZZIN gene are associated with Barth syndrome. Barth syndrome is an X-linked disorder characterized by dilated cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, growth retardation, and 3-methylglutaconic aciduria. The patient's low neutrophil count (1.36*10^9/L) aligns with the neutropenia seen in Barth. Also, the elevated BNP (15,638 pg/ml) and reduced LVEF (36%) support heart failure due to cardiomyopathy.

The genetic testing found a novel splicing mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN. Splicing mutations can lead to abnormal mRNA processing, which would affect the production of the TAFAZZIN protein. This protein is involved in the remodeling of cardiolipin, a key component of mitochondrial membranes. Defects in this process impair mitochondrial function, especially in tissues with high energy demands like the heart, leading to cardiomyopathy.

The blood tests show metabolic acidosis (pH 7.21), which could be due to poor cardiac output leading to tissue hypoxia and lactic acidosis. Elevated creatine kinase might indicate some muscle breakdown, possibly from cardiac stress. The abnormal coagulation could be a result of liver congestion from heart failure or other factors related to critical illness.

Putting it all together: The combination of cardiomyopathy (left ventricular enlargement, reduced ejection fraction), neutropenia, family history of TAFAZZIN mutation, and the identified genetic mutation points strongly towards Barth syndrome. Even though the mother is a carrier without symptoms, X-linked conditions like Barth can present in males who inherit the mutation, as they don't have a second X chromosome to compensate. The prenatal cardiac anomalies detected might have been early signs of the developing cardiomyopathy. The absence of noncompaction on echo is interesting because Barth syndrome is often associated with left ventricular noncompaction, but not all cases present with it, and the thickening here might be part of the hypertrophic response initially.

Other possibilities? Maybe a metabolic disorder causing cardiomyopathy and acidosis, but the genetic mutation here seems conclusive. The presentation fits Barth syndrome well despite some atypical features. The novel splicing variant might be a pathogenic mutation given the clinical context.
results.content:
### Resoning:
<step 1> The patient is a male neonate with severe cyanosis, frothing, and reduced vitality shortly after birth. Prenatal history includes cardiac anomalies and umbilical cord entanglement, prompting early delivery. The presence of a maternal TAFAZZIN mutation raises suspicion for an X-linked disorder, as this gene is linked to Barth syndrome.

<step 2> Echocardiography reveals left atrial/ventricular enlargement, thickened ventricular walls, and severely reduced ejection fraction (36%), consistent with cardiomyopathy. The absence of structural heart defects (e.g., septal defects, outflow tract obstructions) argues against cyanotic congenital heart disease as the primary cause of symptoms.

<step 3> Laboratory findings include neutropenia (1.36*10^9/L), elevated brain natriuretic peptide (15,638 pg/ml), metabolic acidosis, and abnormal coagulation. Neutropenia and cardiomyopathy are hallmark features of Barth syndrome, while elevated BNP reflects heart failure. Metabolic acidosis likely results from poor cardiac output and tissue hypoperfusion.

<step 4> Genetic testing identifies a novel splicing mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.778-30_778del). TAFAZZIN mutations disrupt cardiolipin remodeling, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction in cardiac and immune cells. This mutation, combined with clinical features, strongly supports Barth syndrome, even without left ventricular noncompaction on imaging.

<step 5> Barth syndrome is X-linked recessive, explaining the asymptomatic carrier mother and severe presentation in the male neonate. Rapid postnatal deterioration with cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, and metabolic derangements aligns with this diagnosis, excluding other neonatal causes (e.g., sepsis, respiratory distress syndrome, or metabolic disorders).

### Answer:
Barth syndrome due to a pathogenic TAFAZZIN mutation (c

reasoning_eval.step: The patient is a male neonate with severe cyanosis, frothing, and reduced vitality shortly after birth. Prenatal history includes cardiac anomalies and umbilical cord entanglement, prompting early delivery. The presence of a maternal TAFAZZIN mutation raises suspicion for an X-linked disorder, as this gene is linked to Barth syndrome.

Echocardiography reveals left atrial/ventricular enlargement, thickened ventricular walls, and severely reduced ejection fraction (36%), consistent with cardiomyopathy. The absence of structural heart defects (e.g., septal defects, outflow tract obstructions) argues against cyanotic congenital heart disease as the primary cause of symptoms.

Laboratory findings include neutropenia (1.36*10^9/L), elevated brain natriuretic peptide (15,638 pg/ml), metabolic acidosis, and abnormal coagulation. Neutropenia and cardiomyopathy are hallmark features of Barth syndrome, while elevated BNP reflects heart failure. Metabolic acidosis likely results from poor cardiac output and tissue hypoperfusion.

Genetic testing identifies a novel splicing mutation in intron 10 of TAFAZZIN (c.778-30_778del). TAFAZZIN mutations disrupt cardiolipin remodeling, leading to mitochondrial dysfunction in cardiac and immune cells. This mutation, combined with clinical features, strongly supports Barth syndrome, even without left ventricular noncompaction on imaging.

Barth syndrome is X-linked recessive, explaining the asymptomatic carrier mother and severe presentation in the male neonate. Rapid postnatal deterioration with cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, and metabolic derangements aligns with this diagnosis, excluding other neonatal causes (e.g., sepsis, respiratory distress syndrome, or metabolic disorders).

gt_reasoning_eval.step: Initial considerations included neonatal cardiomyopathies, genetic mitochondrial disorders, infections, and metabolic diseases.

Reasons for considering Barth syndrome (BTHS) included the clinical triad suggestive of BTHS (cardiomyopathy, neutropenia, and 3-methylglutaconic aciduria) and echocardiographic and laboratory findings (dilated cardiomyopathy, low ejection fraction, metabolic acidosis, neutropenia).
Exclusion of infectious causes involved the absence of clinical or laboratory signs of active infection.
Exclusion of non-BTHS cardiomyopathies involved the lack of evidence for alternative specific genetic or acquired causes such as myocarditis or idiopathic cardiomyopathy.
Rarity and specific mutation findings reinforced the likelihood of BTHS.
Diagnostic confirmation involved whole-exome sequencing revealing a novel pathogenic splicing mutation (c.778-30_778del) in the TAFAZZIN gene, inherited in an X-linked pattern.

Final diagnosis: The patient was diagnosed with Barth Syndrome (BTHS) caused by a novel TAFAZZIN splicing site mutation (c.778-30_778del), marking the first genetically confirmed case in a Chinese child. Clinical and genetic findings supported this conclusion.
